name: Epoxy Resin Composites Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Epoxy Resin Composites. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for epoxy resin removal without substrate damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength providing good absorption in epoxy resins with minimal substrate penetration

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise epoxy removal control

repetitionRate.description: High repetition rate for efficient cleaning with thermal management

fluenceThreshold.description: Minimum fluence required for epoxy resin ablation

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of epoxy matrix

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform epoxy removal

passCount.description: Number of cleaning passes for complete epoxy removal

overlapRatio.description: Beam overlap percentage for uniform cleaning coverage

power.description: Typical range for 1064 nm Nd:YAG lasers in polymer composite cleaning to balance ablation efficiency and heat input; starting at 100 W minimizes thermal damage to epoxy matrix. Max safe ~300 W to avoid delamination (based on thermal conductivity ~0.2-0.5 W/mK). Source: Studies in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2018) on laser ablation of CFRP; IPG Photonics YLR series specs.

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds ensure fluence delivery without excessive dwell time; 2000 mm/s optimal for light contamination on epoxy composites to prevent charring. Slower (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy contaminants. Source: Industry guidelines from Laserax equipment manuals; case studies in Journal of Cleaner Production (2020) for surface prep on composites.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred over ps/fs for cost-effective cleaning of polymers; 20 ns minimizes heat-affected zone (HAZ) in low-reflectivity epoxy (~5-10% at 1064 nm). Ps/fs (<1 ns) for ultra-precise but higher cost. Source: Laser physics research in Applied Surface Science (2019) on pulse duration effects in composite ablation.

repetition_rate.description: 20-50 kHz balances cleaning speed and heat accumulation in epoxy (melting point ~150-200°C); higher rates (>50 kHz) risk thermal buildup leading to matrix degradation. Source: Academic literature in Proceedings of SPIE (2021) on pulsed laser cleaning of polymer composites; trade-offs noted in manufacturer data from Coherent Inc.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant removal without substrate ablation; damage threshold for epoxy composites ~1.5-2.5 J/cm² at 1064 nm. Recommended <1.0 J/cm² for safe cleaning. Conservative due to variable fiber content. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Composites Part B (2017); Optics Express (2022).

spot_size.description: Beam diameters for uniform coverage; 200-300 μm recommended for epoxy composites to achieve fine control over ablation on heterogeneous surfaces (e.g., carbon fiber reinforced). Smaller for detail work, larger for broad areas. Source: Equipment specs from Adapt Laser Systems; process optimization in Industrial Laser Solutions (2020).

pass_count.description: Multi-pass (2-5) typical for layered contaminants on epoxy without single-pass overheating; 3 passes recommended for complete removal while preserving composite integrity. Single pass viable for light prep. Source: Industry case studies in Journal of Materials Processing Technology (2019); practical guidelines from laser cleaning service providers.

overlap_ratio.description: 50-70% ensures uniform fluence distribution and avoids gaps/striping on irregular composite surfaces; 60% recommended for epoxy to optimize coverage without excessive energy overlap causing damage. Source: Process optimization studies in Lasers in Manufacturing and Materials Processing (2021); standards from ISO 11553 on laser safety.
